commands.review_summary:
The PR wraps Telegram Bot API transport fetches to add `User-Agent: OpenClawBot/<telegram-plugin-version>` when absent and adds focused fetch tests for default, media source, proxy, and explicit-header paths.

Reproducibility: no. not for the end-to-end ISP throttling path; this review did not reproduce a live Telegram or UK ISP throttle. The source path is reproducible: current main sends Telegram Bot API fetches without a User-Agent wrapper and undici fills a default header when absent.
commands.review_followup: Contributor action is required: add redacted real Telegram or equivalent live transport proof showing the stamped User-Agent after the patch; automation cannot supply the contributor's after-fix setup evidence.

target.title: fix(telegram): stamp bot api user agent
target.body:
Fixes #80446.

## Summary

- Stamps Telegram Bot API outbound fetches with a stable versioned `User-Agent` of `OpenClawBot/<telegram-plugin-version>` when callers have not explicitly provided one.
- Applies the header at the Telegram transport boundary, including direct `transport.sourceFetch` media-download callers and custom proxy fetches.
- Preserves existing proxy selection, dispatcher fallback, abort wrapping, caller-provided dispatchers, and explicit caller `User-Agent` headers.

## Problem summary

Telegram requests were allowed to fall through to the runtime fetch default User-Agent. For Node/undici transports that creates a low-cardinality runtime fingerprint, which the issue reports as a trigger for ISP throttling during sustained Telegram operation.

## Root cause

`resolveTelegramTransport` selected either undici fetch or a caller proxy fetch as the transport source, then called it without normalizing request headers. When no caller header was present, the underlying runtime supplied its own generic default.

## Architectural reasoning

The fix stays inside `extensions/telegram/src/fetch.ts`, which already owns Telegram Bot API transport, proxy dispatchers, sticky fallback, and media-download source fetches. Core fetch behavior and other plugins are unchanged. The wrapper clones request init, preserves dispatcher/body/method/signal fields, uses request-object headers as a base when present, and only supplies OpenClaw's Telegram User-Agent if no caller User-Agent exists.

## Testing performed

- `pnpm test extensions/telegram/src/fetch.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose`
- `pnpm format:check -- extensions/telegram/src/fetch.ts extensions/telegram/src/fetch.test.ts`
- `pnpm check:changed`
- `pnpm lint:extensions -- --threads=1`
- `./node_modules/.bin/tsgo.cmd -p tsconfig.extensions.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/extensions.tsbuildinfo`
- `./node_modules/.bin/tsgo.cmd -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.extensions.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/extensions-test.tsbuildinfo`
- `git diff --check HEAD~1..HEAD`
- `pnpm build` was attempted; bundling and postbuild completed, but the repo wrapper failed at `scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.plugin-sdk.dts.json --declaration true` because this Windows checkout path contains a space. I ran that failing tsgo command directly and it passed.

## Risk analysis

Risk is low. The behavior change is limited to Telegram outbound HTTP headers and only fills in a missing User-Agent. Existing explicit caller headers still win, so custom Bot API proxies can keep their own header policy. Dispatcher fallback and cleanup lifecycle stay on the existing path.

## Backward compatibility

Backward compatible. No config, schema, Gateway protocol, or public plugin SDK surface changes. Existing callers still receive the same transport shape, with request headers enriched only when absent.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Telegram Bot API requests created through the Telegram transport no longer rely on the undici/runtime default User-Agent when no caller User-Agent is supplied.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source checkout on Windows, Node v22.15.0, using the Telegram transport unit tests with mocked undici/custom fetch transports and the real transport wrapper logic.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Called `resolveTelegramFetch` and `resolveTelegramTransport` through `extensions/telegram/src/fetch.test.ts`, then asserted the default undici path, direct `transport.sourceFetch` path, and custom proxy-fetch path all passed `User-Agent: OpenClawBot/2026.5.17` while explicit caller `User-Agent` remained unchanged.

Evidence after fix: `extensions/telegram/src/fetch.test.ts` passed 39/39 tests after the final rebase, including new assertions for default Bot API fetch, direct media source fetch, custom proxy fetch, and explicit caller User-Agent preservation.

Observed result after fix: All tested Telegram outbound transport paths include the versioned OpenClaw User-Agent unless the caller intentionally supplies a different one.

What was not tested: Live Telegram credentials and the reporter's ISP throttling path were not available locally. A broad `pnpm test extensions/telegram -- --reporter=verbose` run also surfaced existing unrelated failures in `extensions/telegram/src/message-cache.test.ts`, which does not import the changed transport module and is outside this patch's surface.
target.author: hemantsudarshan
